ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'beef brisket soup meal set'; closest match is beef soup food product (FOODON_00002473). Meal-set composite not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Beef soup) used as closest defensible match; no specific code exists for a composite meal set including soup, side, and beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept for this composite meal set.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 625 g, comprising a bowl of beef brisket soup with vegetables, a side item, and a beverage, as typically offered at a Western fast-casual restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised (FDC ID 174032); Soup, beef broth or bouillon, canned, ready-to-serve (FDC ID 173168)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact approved-source entry exists for a composite 'Beef Brisket Soup Meal Set' as sold by a fast-casual chain in Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for braised beef brisket (FDC ID 174032) and beef broth/soup (FDC ID 173168), weighted to approximate the meal composition (approximately 35% brisket meat, 50% broth and vegetables, 15% side/drink component). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7, original source: grabfood delivery platform — a forbidden primary source) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data; values are broadly consistent with USDA-derived estimates and have been retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, water, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA composite data for this specific meal configuration is not available.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate for mixed beef-vegetable soup meal.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite beef brisket soup meal set. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogous entries for vegetable-beef soup and mixed meal sets from the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The meal's carbohydrate content (9.23 g/100g) is moderate; the soup broth and vegetable components are expected to have a low-to-medium GI consistent with published values for beef-vegetable soups. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (9.23 g/100g × 625 g / 100 = 57.7 g) / 100 = 30.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 230.8 mg/100g yields approximately 1,442 mg sodium per 625 g serving, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 1,500–2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; soup broth concentration and possible SSB beverage component further elevate risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, avoid consuming the full soup liquid, substitute any sugary beverage with water or unsweetened tea, and limit this meal to occasional consumption given its high per-serving sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content of 9.23 g/100g (57.7 g per serving) with a low estimated GI of 52 is manageable, but the possible inclusion of a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) in the set meal would substantially increase the glycemic load and postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should confirm the beverage is water or an unsweetened drink, request substitution of any high-GI starchy side with lower-GI vegetables, and monitor portion size to keep total carbohydrate intake within their meal plan targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beef brisket is a fatty cut of red meat; saturated fat at 1.54 g/100g (9.6 g per serving) and trans fat at 0.08 g/100g (0.5 g per serving) contribute to LDL cholesterol elevation; cholesterol at 18.46 mg/100g (115 mg per serving) is moderate; sodium load is also a cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat frequency, request lean brisket trimmed of visible fat, avoid the soup broth to reduce sodium intake, and substitute any SSB with water to minimise added sugar and caloric burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef brisket is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acids), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production and may exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef brisket with oily fish or legumes, monitor tolerance to nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, potatoes, bell peppers) that may be present in the soup, and prioritise anti-inflammatory dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket Soup Meal is a Western-style set meal available at fast-casual restaurants in Singapore, most notably associated with Subway's Singapore menu. The meal centres on a bowl of slow-cooked, braised beef brisket served in a savoury, simmered soup broth, typically accompanied by a side item (such as a bread roll or salad) and a beverage. The beef brisket is a tough, flavourful cut from the lower chest of the animal that becomes tender and richly flavoured through prolonged slow cooking or braising. The soup base is typically seasoned with herbs, vegetables (such as carrots, celery, and onions), and savoury condiments, producing a hearty, umami-rich broth. Per 100 g, the meal provides approximately 100 kcal, 6.9 g protein, 3.9 g fat (1.5 g saturated), 9.2 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The meal is moderate in protein and relatively low in fat per 100 g, but the full serving (approximately 625 g) delivers around 625 kcal, 43 g protein, 24 g fat, and 1,442 mg sodium, making sodium management a key clinical consideration. The estimated glycemic index is 52 (low), making it broadly suitable for blood glucose management if the beverage component is non-sugary. Clinically, the primary concerns are the high per-serving sodium load (relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease), the saturated fat and trans fat content from the beef brisket (relevant for cardiovascular disease), and the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid profile of red meat (relevant for rheumatoid arthritis). The meal is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Halal status depends on the specific restaurant and sourcing.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef brisket soup meal?
Is beef brisket soup suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of beef brisket soup
Is beef brisket soup good for high blood pressure?
Can I eat beef brisket soup if I have heart disease?
Is Subway beef brisket soup halal?

confidence_reason: Input record was AI-generated with confidence 0.7 and sourced from a food delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Nutrient values have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for braised beef brisket (FDC ID 174032) and beef broth/soup (FDC ID 173168) and are broadly consistent. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific composite meal set. Medium confidence is assigned because the values are plausible and USDA-consistent but cannot be verified against an exact approved-source entry for this product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174032: beef brisket braised; FDC ID 173168: beef broth/soup) as no exact approved-source entry exists for this composite meal set. Input values (original source: GrabFood delivery platform — a forbidden primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data and found broadly consistent; USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative source of record for this output. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific composite meal set are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-17 for the exact product configuration. 3. Two portions were listed in the input (650 g and 600 g); a midpoint of 625 g is used as the default serving size. 4. Halal status is unconfirmed; the meal contains beef and the halal certification of the specific restaurant chain outlet must be verified independently. 5. The beverage component of the set meal is unspecified; if a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) is included, the sodium, sugar, and glycemic load figures will be materially higher than reported. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable-beef soup and mixed meal sets (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for this product exists in approved sources. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (9.23 g/100g × 625 g / 100 = 57.7 g) / 100 = 30.0.
